summary: DHH celebrates the Arizona House passing HB2005, an anti-monopoly bill that would prevent Apple and Google from forcing developers to use their payment processing systems. He argues this is the most important big tech antitrust victory in twenty years. He dismisses fears that Apple would retaliate by pulling services from Arizona, noting the political and reputational costs would be too high. He points out that Apple has to win in every state while developers only need one victory to crack the dam, and predicts Apple will eventually concede rather than damage its brand.
key_points: Arizona HB2005 passed the House, preventing Apple and Google from mandating their payment processing for app developers
Fears of Apple retaliating by pulling platforms from Arizona are overblown given the political climate and reputational risks
The asymmetry favors developers: Apple must win in all fifty states, but developers only need one state victory to set precedent
Federal antitrust momentum with bipartisan support makes aggressive retaliation even riskier for Apple
The Arizona bill has inspired a growing list of states to pursue similar legislation
money_quotes: The passing of this bill represents the most important victory for big tech antitrust in twenty years.
People assuming that these big tech companies are now so powerful that they don't have to abide by state laws any more.
Are they really going to take kindly to the idea that laws enacted by their elected officials would be met with such a punitive response? By America's most valuable company?
Breaking them up is a mainstream idea with supporters on both sides.
Apple and Google shouldn't be able to put a 30% tax on the digital economy!
Apple has to win potentially fifty times, if all fifty states eventually take a swing at this, whereas app developers just need to win one state to crack the dam.
Whereas Apple used to be able to largely dodge out of the Big Bad Tech conversation, they're now smack in the thick of it.
key_insight: The asymmetric nature of state-by-state antitrust battles means Apple must win everywhere while developers only need one breakthrough, making the eventual end of the 30% app store tax increasingly likely.
